coredata.dc:title: Novel fluorescent recoverable probe based on carbon quantum dots/polypyrrole composite for the simultaneous determination of chromium(VI) and sulfite
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zhanchao
Chen, Lianhua
Wang, Qiaoe
Yang, Rongguang
Hu, Xiao
Liu, Hongwei
Li, Jingyong
Liu, Yan
coredata.dc:description: A novel carbon quantum dots/polypyrrole (CDs/PPy) composite fluorescence probe with excellent selectivity and high fluorescent stability was successfully designed and fabricated by microwave method. PPy was selected as the surface passivating agent to improve the quantum yield (QY) of CDs, which has been demonstrated having a QY enhancement effect, for CDs, from the original value of 12.79% up to 43.68% (CDs/PPy). The fluorescence of CDs/PPy can be effectively quenched by chromium(VI) (Cr(VI)) via inner filter effect (IFE). Importantly, upon addition of sulfite (SO3 2−), the fluorescence intensity of the CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system was recovered through the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) and inhibited the IFE process between CDs/PPy and Cr(VI). Accordingly, the efficient composite fluorescent probe was established to determine Cr(VI) in aqueous solution and the representative reductive substance SO3 2− in food. The excellent determination performance and satisfied linear response ranges of the proposed fluorescence probe were obtained over the range of 0.01-250.00 µmol/L and 5.00-250.00 µmol/L with the low detection limits of 2.40 nmol/L and 121.00 nmol/L for Cr(VI) and the representative reducing substance SO3 2−, respectively. Meanwhile, the fluorescence probe has been successfully applied in determination of Cr(VI) and SO3 2− in real samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots/polypyrrole
Fluorescent probe
Chromium(VI)
Inner filter effect
Determination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the CDs/PPy fluorescent probes
Determination of Cr(VI) and SO3 2− in real samples
FT-IR, Particle size distribution and TEM analysis
Determination of Cr(VI) and SO3 2− in real samples

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22178151 ). the Open Research Fund Program of Key Laboratory of Cosmetic, China National Light Industry, Beijing Technology and Business University ( KLC-2020-YB10 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Entire XPS scanning spectrum of CDs-PPy (a) and the high-resolution XPS of C1s (b), N1s (c), and O1s.
The FT-IR spectra of CDs, PPy and CDs/PPy composite.
Particle size distribution of CDs prepared at different temperature (120 °C (a), 150 °C (b), 180 °C (c), and 200 °C (d)) and The TEM images of CDs (e) and CDs/PPy composite (f).
The excitation-dependent spectra of CDs (a), and The emission spectra of CDs, PPy and CDs/PPy composite when excited at 350 nm (b).
The emission spectra of CDs/PPy composite at different pH values (a) and The fluorescent intensity stability of CDs/PPy composite in 30 days (b).

Fluorescence responses of CDs/PPy in the presence of different concentration of Cr(VI) (a) and the relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and Cr(VI) concentration (b) with inserted the linear relationship plot between (F0-F)/F0 and concentration.

Fluorescence responses of CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system in the presence of different concentration of SO3 2− (a) and the relationship between (F-F0)/F0 and SO3 2−concentration (b) with inserted the linear relationship plot between (F-F0)/F0 and concentration.

The UV-vis absorbance spectra of CDs, PPy and CDs/PPy composite (a), The UV-vis absorbance spectra of CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system in the presence of SO3 2− in different concentration (b), The fluorescent responses of CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system with Cr(VI) and other reductant added (c), Fluorescence responses of CDs/PPy composite towards Cr(VI) and various ions (d).
Schematic illustration of fluorescence “on-off” assay for Cr(VI) and “off-on” assay for SO3 2−.
The comparison of different determination methods towards Cr(VI) or SO3 2−.

head.ce:title: Novel fluorescent recoverable probe based on carbon quantum dots/polypyrrole composite for the simultaneous determination of chromium(VI) and sulfite

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Civil Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dCollege of Civil Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel carbon quantum dots/polypyrrole (CDs/PPy) composite fluorescence probe with excellent selectivity and high fluorescent stability was successfully designed and fabricated by microwave method. PPy was selected as the surface passivating agent to improve the quantum yield (QY) of CDs, which has been demonstrated having a QY enhancement effect, for CDs, from the original value of 12.79% up to 43.68% (CDs/PPy). The fluorescence of CDs/PPy can be effectively quenched by chromium(VI) (Cr(VI)) via inner filter effect (IFE). Importantly, upon addition of sulfite (SO3 2−), the fluorescence intensity of the CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system was recovered through the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) and inhibited the IFE process between CDs/PPy and Cr(VI). Accordingly, the efficient composite fluorescent probe was established to determine Cr(VI) in aqueous solution and the representative reductive substance SO3 2− in food. The excellent determination performance and satisfied linear response ranges of the proposed fluorescence probe were obtained over the range of 0.01-250.00 µmol/L and 5.00-250.00 µmol/L with the low detection limits of 2.40 nmol/L and 121.00 nmol/L for Cr(VI) and the representative reducing substance SO3 2−, respectively. Meanwhile, the fluorescence probe has been successfully applied in determination of Cr(VI) and SO3 2− in real samples with satisfactory results.

Schematic illustration of fluorescence “on-off” assay for Cr(VI) and “off-on” assay for SO3 2−
Image, graphical abstract
.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium compounds are widely used in various industrial applications, such as paint pigment, electroplating, leather tanning, textiles, dye production, coating manufacturing, and other industrial processes [1–3]. Meanwhile, chromium contamination in industrial discharge waters and contaminated groundwater have also become one of the most significant environmental problems today. Especially, hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) species pose a threat to ecosystems and human health due to their greater genotoxicity, mutagenicity, carcinogenicity, mobility and solubility than trivalent chromium (Cr(III)) species [4–6]. According to the regulation of the World Health Organization (WHO), the maximum permissible concentration of Cr(VI) in drinking water should be less than 50.0 µg/L [7]. Therefore, it is of great necessity to determine the concentration of Cr(VI) in aqueous solution. On the other hand, according to the reported literatures, there are many reductive substances in our daily life, such as ascorbic acid, hydrogen sulfide and dopamine, can be simultaneously determined with Cr(VI) by probes on account of oxidation-reduction reaction [8–10]. Based on this, sulfite, as a representative reducing substance in food and beverage industries, has been profusely used as additive, which is not only because of its multifaceted role as preservative, antibacterial agent, bleaching agent but also due to the prevent oxidation and enzymatic reactions [11–13]. However, sulfite not only damage aquatic communities by seriously depleting the amount of oxygen in water, but is harmful towards the human body including respiratory diseases, cardiovascular diseases and many neurological disorders [14–16]. Thus, the levels of sulfites have been strictly controlled, which is regulated as 0.7 mg/kg of body weight for our acceptable daily intake [17], and it is also important for sulfite determination in order to ensure the safety and quality of food. Therefore, considering the widespread and toxic effects of them, developing a fast, simple and highly sensitive and selective method for sensing Cr(VI) and SO3 2- in the environmental aqueous solution and our daily food is extremely urgent and important.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), acting as a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with photoluminescence, have attracted tremendous attention as one of the most interesting and potential candidates for sensors or probes because of its good water solubility, tunable fluorescence, strong photochemical stability, easy surface modifications and excellent biocompatibility [18–20]. However, in the judicious process and practical applications, there remain significant challenges of low quantum yield, easy contamination, and poor reproducibility for CDs [21]. In order to fully implement these requisites, methods of achieving defined and specific functionality on material produced using scalable methods must be developed [22,23]. Therefore, the surface passivation is commonly used in improving the performance of CDs as an ideal method based on this respect, which makes CDs possible to achieve higher selectivity, higher fluorescence quantum yield, and better optical stability [24]. Many organic molecules, oligomer and polymers could be served as the surface passivating agents to prepare quantum dots with improved quantum yield (QY). For instance, Shen et al. facilely prepared fluorescent sensor based on polypyrrole/graphene quantum dots (PPy/GQDs) core/shell hybrids for dopamine (DA) detection with high sensitivity and selectivity through a photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process. The composite exhibited the enhancement of fluorescence intensity with existence of PPy [25]. In this regard, polypyrrole (PPy), which is an important and bio-friendly conducting polymer, can also be amenable to functionalization before and after polymerization and has high chemical and environmental stability and electrically conducting [26,27]. Especially, PPy possesses the similar π-conjugated structure with CDs, and the N heteroatom on pyrrole ring may cause the doping effect to tune the photoluminescence (PL) of the materials [28,29]. Hence, it is worthwhile to fabricate CDs/PPy composite fluorescent probes for the selective quantitative determination in the presence of multiple ions and molecules. Significantly, to our best knowledge, there are few reports on the simultaneous determination of chromium(VI) and sulfite based on inner filter effect (IFE) and oxidation-reduction reaction process using CDs/PPy composite fluorescence probe.

In the present work, PPy was used as the surface passivating regent to fabricate a novel selective and fluorescent stable CDs/PPy composite fluorescence probe with a favorable QY (43.68%). The morphology and structure of the resultant materials were characterized by Fourier transmission infrared spectra (FT-IR), Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and Fluorescence spectrometer. The obtained fluorescence probe was firstly used to determine Cr(VI) based on IFE. Meanwhile, the SO3 2− was also determined because the fluorescence intensity of the CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system was recovered through the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) and inhibit the IFE process between CDs/PPy and Cr(VI) in the presence of SO3 2−. Furthermore, to demonstrate the practicability of the method developed, the fabricated CDs/PPy fluorescent probes were applied for the determination of Cr(VI) and SO3 2− in different real samples.

Ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), glycerin, potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) and sodium sulfite (Na2SO3) were purchased from Guoyao Chemical Reagents Corp (Shanghai, China). Pyrrole, ascorbic acid (AA), catechol, polyethylene glycol 1500 (PEG 1500) were purchased from the Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals used were of analytical grade. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

Fourier transmission infrared spectra (FT-IR, 4000-400 cm−1) in KBr were recorded on a NICOLET NEXUS 4700 FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) analysis was performed by using JEM-2010HR at 200 kV (JEOL, Japan). Hitachi F-4500 Fluorescence Spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan) was employed to investigate the fluorescence property of the prepared materials in this work. A pHS-3C digital pH meter (LIDA Instrument Factory, Shanghai, China) was used for the pH value adjustments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the CDs/PPy fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: PPy was synthesized according to the reported literature [25]. Briefly, 0.1 g FeCl2·4H2O and 1.0 mL pyrrole were added to 94.0 mL deionized water. Afterwards, 5.0 mL H2O2 was gradually dropped into the above mixture under stirring and continually reacted for 6.0 h in this condition. After the reaction, the contents were cooled to room temperature, recovered by centrifugation. In order to remove by-products and unreacted ingredients the products were extensively washed with deionized water for several times. At last, PPy can be obtained by vacuum freeze drying.

The CDs/PPy were prepared by the following method. 1.0 g of PEG 1500, 0.5 g of serine and 15.0 mL of glycerine were uniformly mixed and kept stirring for 1.0 h. Afterwards, the above mixture was put into microwave irradiation at 600 W and reacted 8.0 min. 100.0 mL of deionized water and 100.0 µL of PPy (5.0 mg/mL) were then gradually added to the solution with constantly stirred under the room temperature and the process will maintain for 1.0 h. Subsequently, the resultant mixture was completely purified through the intercept by a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 3500 Da. Finally, the CDs/PPy solution was obtained and stored at 4 °C for further use. Meanwhile, the pure CDs were also prepared by the same synthesis process as CDs/PPy solution but without adding PPy after the microwave irradiation. Additionally, the pure CDs were obtained by vacuum freeze drying.

In order to study the excitation dependence and optimal excitation wavelength of CDs, the CDs were excited at different wavelengths in the range of 300-400 nm. It is observed that the optimal excitation wavelength and the corresponding optimal emission wavelength are 350 nm and 430 nm, respectively. Subsequently, the quantum yield of CDs and CDs/PPy were measured and calculated according to the previous article [30]. The equation used is as follows:
(1)
Φ
=
Φ
R
×
F S
A R
η S 2
F R
A S
η R 2
where
Φ
is the fluorescence quantum yield, S represents the sample, R is the reference compound, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, F is the integrated fluorescence intensity under the fluorescence emission spectrum, and η is the refractive index.

The stability of CDs/PPy in different values of pH (pH=4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0) was firstly investigated, which was conducted by 0.05 mol/L phosphate buffer. Subsequently, to achieve the determination of Cr(VI) with fluorescence spectra, 1.0 mL of CDs/PPy solution and 2.0 mL of 0.05 mol/L phosphate buffer (pH=4.0) were mixed homogeneously, followed by the addition of 1.0 mL of different concentrations of Cr(VI) (0.0-10.0 mmol/L). After completely mixed, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

In the determination of SO3 2−, 1.0 mL of CDs/PPy solution was uniformly mixed with 1.0 mL of 0.05 mol/L phosphate buffer (pH=4.0) containing 1.0 mL of 1.0 mmol/L Cr(VI) solution to build CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system. Afterwards, the SO3 2− solutions with different concentrations (0.0-8.0 mmol/L) were added to the above mixture. The fluorescence detection was performed after uniform vibration with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Additionally, to better study the practicability and university of the obtained probe in oxidation-reduction systems, the four other reducing agents including sulfion (S2−), ascorbic acid (AA), nitrite ion (NO2 −) and catechol were selected in this investigation. The concentration of all the substances was 50.0 µmol/L, and the determination conditions were the same as mentioned above.

To evaluate the selectivity of CDs/PPy fluorescent probes for Cr(VI), the interfering ions were prepared (Cr(III), Na+, K+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl−, Br−, CO3 2, SO4 2−, NO3 −) with concentration of 20.0 µmol/L under the same conditions. Then, 1.0 mL of interfering ions solution was added to the mixed solution of CDs/PPy solution (1.0 mL) and phosphate buffer (2.0 mL, pH=4.0) to determine the corresponding emission spectra and fluorescence of each group in parallel experiments under the optimal excitation conditions. The intensity change was compared with the emission spectrum when Cr(VI) was detected under the same conditions, and the selectivity of the composite probe for Cr(VI) can be obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cr(VI) and SO3 2− in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the standard addition method was adopted to determine the concentration of Cr(VI) and SO3 2− in real water samples and beverage by the CDs/PPy system. The tap water and river water samples were collected from the laboratory and Yudai River (Zhenjiang, China), respectively. The glucose solution, white spirits and grape juice were obtained from the native supermarket. And they were all filtered through a 0.45 µm Millipore membrane. Before detection, the beverage samples were had to elute 100 times and the concentration of the samples was analyzed by fluorescence spectra. All the measured solutions were excited at 350 nm. Subsequently, the concentration of the real samples could be obtained according to fluorescence spectra.

The XPS measurements is carried out to determine the composition and functional groups of CDs/PPy. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the entire XPS spectra display that the C1s peak at around 284.8 eV, the O1s peak at about 532.8 eV, and the N1s peak at near 399.5 eV are observed in the composite probe. The high-resolution C1s and O1s spectrum (Fig. 1(b and d)) confirm the existence of the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, such as C=O (286.3 eV), C-O (285.8 eV), and C-OH (288.5 eV) groups. Meanwhile, in Fig. 1(c), the apparent binding energy peaks centered at 399.8 eV and 399.3 eV in the N 1s spectrum are attributed to N-H and C-N species, respectively, confirming the presence of nitrogen-contained groups in CDs/PPy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR, Particle size distribution and TEM analysis
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR spectroscopy is firstly employed to characterize the structural changes of starting materials and chemical groups present on the surface of the different samples. All the details are displayed in Fig. 2 . The observed features around 3400 and 1080 cm−1 performing both in CDs and CDs/PPy correspond the stretching variations of -OH, while the band at 2950 and 1380 cm−1 suggest the existence of aromatic the stretching vibration and bending vibration of C-H groups. In the spectra of CDs and CDs/PPy composite, the adsorption peaks at 1670 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of C=C and C=O, respectively. The absorption at about 1690 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 in PPy and CDs/PPy are associated with the stretching vibrations of C=O and C-N bounds, respectively. All the above phenomena prove the evidence that the hydroxyl and amino groups groups exist in the surface of CDs/PPy.

In order to obtain the favorable performance CDs/PPy composite, the particle size distribution and quantum yields of are investigated at different temperature, which can be seen in Fig. 3 . As performed in Fig. 3(a)-(d), particle size distribution of CDs is more uniform at the temperature 150 °C and 180 °C. Meanwhile, after calculated, the quantum yields of CDs prepared at 120, 150, 180 and 200 °C aare 4.61%, 12.08%, 12.97% and 9.26%, respectively. Comprehensively, 180 °C is selected as the optimized temperature to prepare CDs.

The TEM images of CDs and CDs/PPy are also shown in Fig. 3. As displayed in Fig. 3(e), the average size of CDs is less than 5.0 nm. It can be clearly seen in Fig. 3(f) that PPy microspheres are loaded with CDs nanoparticles and the edge of CDs/PPy is bright. These images demonstrate that CDs load on the rough surface of PPy and the CDs/PPy composite is successfully synthesized. Also, the size of CDs is much smaller than PPy microspheres and there is a tendency that CDs gather in the surface of PPy and load by adsorption. All the above phenomena indicate that the CDs/PPy composite has been successfully synthesized.

The Zeta potential is adopted to measure the Zeta of CDs and CDs/PPy composite. The negative Zeta potential value of CDs and CDs/PPy composite are -2.22 mV and -13.7 mV, respectively, indicating that the electronegativity of CDs/PPy composite is stronger than CDs. In addition, because of electrostatic repulsion, the stable and uniform distribution of CDs in solution prevents them from gathering together due to quantum confinement effect. Consequently, the fluorescence of CDs could be more stable. The Zeta potential of CDs/PPy is more negative than that of CDs owing to a certain amount of N and the π conjugated bond. Meanwhile, CDs/PPy composite has more free electrons, making the Zeta potential of CDs/PPy more negative and the dispersion of CDs/PPy system more uniform and stable than CDs.

The fluorescence of CDs and CDs/PPy has been investigated in detail. CDs are excited at different excitation wavelength from 300 to 400 nm. The optimal emission wavelength and intensity are recorded by fluorescence spectrometry analysis. Fig. 4 (a) shows the excitation-dependent spectra of CDs. It can be seen that the emission wavelength of CDs become longer with the increasing of excitation wavelength while the fluorescence emission intensity increases initially and then decreases. The optimal excitation wavelength and emission wavelength are 350 nm and 430 nm, respectively.

Fig. 4(b) shows the emission spectra of CDs and CDs/PPy composite when excited at 350 nm. As shown in it, the optimal fluorescence emission wavelength of CDs and the pure PPy are 430 nm and 425 nm, respectively. But the CDs/PPy composite shows a red shift to 450 nm, which may originate from the diameter of particles of CDs/PPy becomes bigger after the composite. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of CDs/PPy composite is stronger than CDs for the reason that the more uniform distribution of CDs resulting from the combination of CDs and PPy make the aggregation of CDs dropped. This result is consistent with Zeta analysis.

Furthermore, the effect of pH value on the fluorescence properties of CDs/PPy composite was investigated. Under the same concentrations, fluorescence of CDs/PPy composite were detected at different pH of 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0 and 8.0. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), the fluorescent intensity of CDs/PPy composite is very stable over a wide range of pH values. This might be due to the large amounts of hydroxyl and a small amount of carboxyl and functional groups containing N in the obtained composite.

Fig. 5(b) illustrates the fluorescent intensity stability of CDs/PPy composite in 30 days under the optimal excitation conditions. As performed in the figure, no remarkable change of fluorescence intensity of CDs/PPy composite could be observed, which demonstrates that the fluorescent intensity of CDs/PPy composite is very stable for its uniform and stable dispersion in solution. Thus, CDs/PPy composite can be used to construct compound fluorescent probes for detection.

In the work, the excellent selective and high fluorescent stable CDs/PPy composite fluorescence probe is constructed and applied simultaneously determine chromium(VI) and sulfite based on inner filter effect (IFE) and oxidation-reduction reaction process. PPy is selected as the surface passivating agent to improve the QY of CDs. The QY of CDs/PPy and CDs are determined and calculated using Eq. (1), and their specific values (Qx) are 43.68% and 12.79%, respectively, demonstrating that the QY is enhanced prominently after CDs passivated with PPy. The phenomena may be explained by the theory that PPy possesses the similar π-conjugated structure with CDs, and the N heteroatom on pyrrole ring may cause the doping effect to tune the PL, leading to the formation of an uncommon surface states [24]. As seen from Scheme 1 , it can be found that the strong fluorescence of CDs/PPy fluorescent probes could be efficiently turned off in the presence of Cr(VI) due to the inner filter effect (IFE). IFE, as an excellent alternative way to design fluorescent assays, is known to result from the absorption of the exciting and/or emitted light by absorbers in the detection system [31,32]. Comparing to other mechanisms, this method could achieve an intensive sensitivity since the changes in the absorbance of sensors transform exponentially into fluorescence intensity changes. After addition of SO3 2−, Cr(VI) is reduced to Cr(III) which has nearly no influence on the fluorescence of CDs/PPy and the fluorescence is recovered. Thus, the CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system could be a “turn-on” fluorescence sensor for the detection of SO3 2−.

The fluorescence responses of CDs/PPy in the presence of different concentration of Cr(VI) are observed (Fig. 6 (a)). Then, the standard curve between the quenching degree of fluorescence intensity (F0-F)/F0 and the change of Cr(VI) concentration is built to investigate the fluorescence change of probe. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the fluorescence intensity is gradually decreased to completely quenched with increasing of Cr(VI) concentration. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the quenching degree of CDs/PPy composite fluorescent probes was on the rise first and then tended to be stable, which indicates that CDs/PPy composite fluorescent probes can be used as Cr(VI) fluorescence probes of fluorescence quenching. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0-F)/F0 is proportional to the concentration of Cr(VI) in the range from 0.1 to 250.0 µmol/L whose curve are fitted to the linear regression equation of Y=0.0021x +0.03488, R2=0.9975. Hence, the limit of detection (LOD) for Cr(VI) based on 3σ/k is 2.4 nmol/L (where k is the slope of calibration curve, σ is the standard deviation of 10 blank measurements). Consequently, CDs/PPy composite fluorescent probes are simple and convenient for Cr(VI) determination.

The CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system is conducted for further fluorescence detection of a series of different concentrations of SO3 2− after Cr(VI) determination. As shown in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence intensity of CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system recovers gradually with the increasing of SO3 2− concentration. The inset of Fig. 7(b) represents the linear relationship between the fluorescence enhancement factors (F−F0)/F0 and the concentration of SO3 2− in the range of 0.5 to 500.0 µmol/L. The equation of regression is Y=0.00371x+0.09506, with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9969 and a limit of detection (LOD) of 121.0 nmol/L.

The UV-vis absorption spectra of CDs, PPy and K2Cr2O7 were detected (Fig. 8(a)) to study the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CDs/PPy composite fluorescent probes in Cr(VI) detection. The results show a spectral overlap near the optimal fluorescence excitation wavelength at 350 nm of CDs. Therefore, fluorescence quenching based on the IFE occurred. When the selectivity determination of CDs/PPy composite fluorescent probes towards Cr(VI) is investigated, the presence of other ions does not change the fluorescence properties in the assay system because of the IFE peculiarity of Cr(VI), while other ions do not possess this property.

To study the mechanism of fluorescence recovery of the system for SO3 2− assay, the UV-vis absorbance spectra of CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system in the presence of SO3 2− in different concentrations aree further measured. In Fig. 8(b), there is no UV absorption at 350 nm without Cr(VI) in CDs/PPy system. Upon addition of equal Cr(VI), the UV absorption of CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system gradually weakens to almost no existence with the increasing concentration of SO3 2−. Hence, the content of Cr (VI) is gradually decreasing in SO3 2− detection. We can speculate that the oxidizing Cr(VI) is consumed due to the oxidation-reduction reaction between Cr(VI) and SO3 2−. And then the IFE is weakened and the fluorescence of CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system recovers. In order to further verify the speculation, the S2−, AA, NO2− and catechol aree explored at the same CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system. As shown in Fig. 8(c), the fluorescence recovery remains occurred. The result clearly demonstrates that the CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system can be successfully applied to detect varieties of reductant because of the mechanism of oxidation-reduction reaction.

The fluorescence intensity of CDs/PPy composite has been investigated in the presence of other ions (Cr(III), Na+, K+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl−, Br−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, Cu2+, Fe3+) under the same conditions. The fluorescence intensity of CDs/PPy composite probes was recorded and compared with the results of Cr(VI) detection in the presence of other ions. It could be seen in Fig. 8 (d) obviously that the fluorescence responses of CDs/PPy composite have no obvious change except Cr(VI). Even existence of the colored metal ions Fe3+ and Cu2+, it also displays an acceptable selectivity for the target ion. All the results indicate that the CDs/PPy composite shows good selectivity for Cr(VI) detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cr(VI) and SO3 2− in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the practicality of CDs/PPy composite fluorescent probes for Cr(VI) detection, the standard addition method was employed to determine the Cr(VI) concentration in tap water samples and river water samples. The results of recovery are shown in Table S1. The recovery of samples is 99.7-101.2% and relative standard deviation (RSD) is 0.62-0.93%. These results indicate that CDs/PPy composite fluorescent probes have excellent precision and accuracy for the detection of Cr(VI).

The practicality of CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system for SO3 2− detection in real samples (glucose solution, liqueur and grape juice) was further evaluated. The standard method was adopted to detect SO3 2− content in realistic samples. As shown in Table S2, the recovery is from 97.1% to 104.5% and RSD is 1.08-2.13%. The results above suggest that the great precision and accuracy of CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system for SO3 2− detection so that it can be well applied to the determination of SO3 2− in real samples.

The comparison between this method and other reported material based quantum dot methods for Cr(VI) or SO3 2- detection in a linear range and LODs is summarized in Table 1 . As can be seen, the proposed method had lower LOD and higher precision. Therefore, the CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system can be applied to the quantitative detection for both Cr(VI) and SO3 2−.

In this work, the novel selective and fluorescent stable carbon quantum dots/polypyrrole (CDs/PPy) composite fluorescence probe was successfully fabricated. PPy was selected as the surface passivating agent to improve the quantum yield (QY) of CDs, which has been demonstrated having a QY enhancement effect, for CDs, from the original value of 12.79% up to 43.68% (CDs/PPy). The obtained fluorescence probe was demonstrated to effectively achieve the determination of Cr(VI) based on IFE quenching mechanism. Upon addition of SO3 2−, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system was recovered through the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) and inhibit the IFE process between CDs/PPy and Cr(VI). Accordingly, the efficient composite fluorescent probe was successfully established to determine Cr(VI) and SO3 2−. The excellent determination performance and satisfied linear response ranges of the proposed fluorescence probe were obtained over the range of 0.01-250.00 µmol/L and 5.00-250.00 µmol/L with the low detection limits of 2.40 nmol/L and 121.00 nmol/L for Cr(VI) and SO3 2−, respectively. The CDs/PPy-Cr(VI) system can be successfully applied to detect varieties of reductant because of the mechanism of oxidation-reduction reaction. Meanwhile, the fluorescence probe has been successfully applied in determination of Cr(VI) and SO3 2− in real samples with satisfactory results.

Zhanchao Liu: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Lianhua Chen: Investigation, Validation. Qiaoe Wang: Writing – review & editing, Software. Rongguang Yang: Investigation. Xiao Hu: Validation, Software. Hongwei Liu: Project administration, Software. Jingyong Li: Resources, Formal analysis. Yan Liu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22178151). the Open Research Fund Program of Key Laboratory of Cosmetic, China National Light Industry, Beijing Technology and Business University (KLC-2020-YB10).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2021.131409.
